learning_outcomes.description: Appraise the theory and practice of genome informatics and be prepared to apply these methods to address current problems in the field.
Compare the different types of available genomic and transcriptomic data and critique the general approaches used to analyse these.
Examine the principles of sequence alignment to the practical problem of mapping reads to a reference sequence.
Judge the approaches required for the analysis of metagenomic data.
Appraise the principles underpinning common de novo assembly approaches.
Explain the approaches required for phylogenomic analysis of sequence data.
Describe the approaches required for genomic analysis and functional annotation of human genomic sequence data.

assessments.due_date:
Each workshop in Module 1 and Module 2 introduces a new assignment (except the introductory workshop in week 1) 3/08/2026 - 14/09/2026

The preliminary exercises in each assignment are required to be completed during the relevant workshop (weeks 2-7).
assessments.task_description:
This assessment item aims to provide students with an opportunity to connect practical and conceptual issues relevant to the course. 

SIX In-lab assignments, each related to one of the 6 module 1 and module 2 workshops (weeks 2-7) will be introduced during the related workshop session. These assignments are formative and will aid you in learning the key command-line approaches required for analysing high-throughput sequence data.

Assignments include preliminary practical exercises that will be completed in class. A passmark will be awarded for an assignment by showing your tutor a genuine attempt at the preliminary exercises before leaving the workshop session.

Each assignment also includes additional exercises that may be completed during the workshop session or in your own time. Answers to each workshop will be given before the beginning of the next workshop.

As the assignments are formative in nature, no extensions or deferral is permitted.

Understanding of the approaches and concepts covered by each assignment will be assessed as part of the final end-of-semester examination.

Workshop absences

You must complete 100% of the practical component of this course (Assessment Hurdles - under Additional Course Grading Information).

If you cannot complete your timetabled workshop session because of circumstances beyond your control (e.g., illness), you must contact me by email and attach supporting documentation that covers the date of the missed workshop. Applications must be received within 24 hours of your practical session.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Physical submission of completed assignments is not required.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: ASSESSMENT 2: De novo assembly and phylogenomics
assessments.due_date:
6/10/2026 2:00 pm

The technical report is due before 2pm Tuesday October 6th 2026. The oral interview will take place during the normal workshop time, between 2pm and 5:50pm Thursday October 8th 2026 (week 10).
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution
assessments.task_description:
Students will be given a real set of high-throughput sequencing data and will be required to undertake

1) de novo assembly,

2) assessment of assembly quality,

3) ab initio gene prediction, and

4) phylogenomic analyses.

 

The assessment will consist of two components:

(A) a brief technical report with recommendation(s) submitted before the week 10 workshop

(B) a short (5 minute) oral interview during the week 10 workshop based on the technical report and other related Module 3 content.

Guidelines for preparing the technical report and the scope of the interview will be given on Blackboard.


Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the technical report component of this assessment will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

For the interview component, this assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1

assessments.task_description:
Students will analyse and interpret metagenomic sequence data in the context of real-world scenarios. Analysis will include sequence simulation, sequence quality control, taxonomic assignment, and annotation using both web-based and command-line tools. Students will prepare answers to a problem as well as a report detailing the bioinformatic analyses of a viral metagenome and a description of the viral community. Full guidelines for the project report requirements will be given on Learn.UQ.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your report through the course Turnitin submission point in Blackboard. Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin please name your file in the following way. FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1Submission via Turnitin portal on Blackboard @ Learn.UQ

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 4(27 Jul - 23 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 1: Read mapping and the command-line [SB]

Workshop sessions each week include lectures, practical exercise walk-throughs and independent work on the topic of read-mapping. Includes in-depth introduction to unix command-line. Workshop exercises carried out in-person during the workshops in week 2, 3 and 4 will form part of the Assessment 1.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
